statement: Samtök iðnaðarins (SI, Federation of Icelandic Industries) supports EU membership, arguing it would improve market access, reduce regulatory costs, and attract foreign investment in manufacturing and technology. SI's members include Iceland's aluminium smelters (operated by Alcoa, Rio Tinto, and Century Aluminum), which currently export duty-free to the EU under the EEA but face regulatory uncertainty regarding the EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM). SI argues that EU membership would: (1) give Iceland a seat at the table for industrial policy decisions affecting its energy-intensive industries; (2) provide access to EU R&D funding (Horizon Europe) as a full participant rather than an associated country; (3) reduce trade friction for manufactured goods beyond the single market.
source_name: Samtök iðnaðarins — Annual report 2025; SI policy briefs on CBAM and Horizon Europe
caveats: Iceland's manufacturing sector is dominated by aluminium smelting, which is capital-intensive and employs relatively few workers. SI's pro-EU position may not reflect the views of smaller industrial firms. The CBAM issue could be addressed through bilateral negotiations without full EU membership.

related_entries.statement: Samtök atvinnulífsins (SA, Confederation of Icelandic Enterprise) is the most prominent organisational supporter of EU membership. SA's position paper (2025) argues that membership would: (1) reduce the cost of capital by eliminating currency risk premium (~2–3 percentage points on interest rates), (2) improve regulatory predictability for businesses operating across borders, (3) provide access to EU structural funds for infrastructure, and (4) strengthen Iceland's negotiating position in international trade. SA represents approximately 2,000 companies employing 70% of private sector workers. However, SA's fisheries-sector members (through SFS) have dissented from the pro-EU position, creating internal tension. SA has pledged ISK 200 million (~€1.3 million) to the pro-EU campaign.

Iceland has among the lowest electricity prices in Europe for industrial consumers, at approximately €30–40 per MWh, compared to the EU-27 average of €120–150 per MWh (2024). Household electricity prices are also well below European averages at roughly €0.08/kWh versus the EU average of €0.25/kWh. This cheap, abundant renewable electricity has attracted energy-intensive industries — aluminium smelting alone consumes approximately 70% of Iceland's electricity output. EU internal energy market rules, if fully applied, could create pressure to allow electricity price convergence with continental Europe, potentially raising domestic prices to fund interconnector infrastructure.

citations.report_title: Iðnaðurinn forherðist í andstöðu gegn ESB

claims.claim_text: Samtök iðnaðarins hafa ekki tekið formlega afstöðu til ESB-aðildar Íslands.
Meirihluti félagsmanna Samtaka iðnaðarins er andvígur ESB-aðild þótt samtökin sjálf hafi ekki tekið formlega afstöðu.

citations.report_title: Þrír hópar sem myndu hagnast mest á aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu

claims.claim_text: Umræða um verndaraðgerðir ESB vegna kísilmálms síðasta haust sýndi að EES-aðild tryggir ekki að íslensk fyrirtæki séu meðhöndluð sem fullgildir aðilar að ráðstöfunum á innri markaðnum.

citations.report_title: „Við hefðum náð meiri árangri með því að vera innan Evrópusambandsins“

claims.claim_text: Evrópusambandið er að dýpka innbyrðis samstarf sitt og verja hagsmuni sinna fyrirtækja vegna þróunar heimsmála.
